perturbation.original_text: VerticalNet shall design, develop and implement a Co-Branded Career Center and shall use commercially reasonable efforts to implement the Co-Branded Career Center as soon as possible following the Effective Date, and in any event, no later than February 1, 2000.
perturbation.changed_text: VerticalNet shall endeavor to design, develop and possibly implement a Co-Branded Career Center and shall consider using commercially reasonable efforts to implement the Co-Branded Career Center when feasible following the Effective Date, with a target implementation date of around February 1, 2000.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a clear obligation for VerticalNet to design, develop, and implement the Co-Branded Career Center. The modified text introduces significant ambiguity by replacing 'shall' with weaker terms like 'endeavor' and 'consider,' and by qualifying 'commercially reasonable efforts' with 'when feasible.' It replaces the deadline 'no later than February 1, 2000' with a more vague 'around February 1, 2000' removing any hard deadline.
perturbation.law_citation: Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes Title 42 Pa.C.S.A. § 8332. Enforcement of contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts contract law in Pennsylvania by making the obligation to create the Co-Branded Career Center so vague and discretionary that it would be difficult to enforce in court. The original clause provided a clear obligation and a deadline. The altered version allows VerticalNet to potentially avoid fulfilling the obligation under the contract, hindering Neoforma's ability to claim a breach of contract. Under 42 Pa.C.S.A. § 8332, contract enforcement relies on the clarity and definiteness of the contractual terms. This modification severely undermines that.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 8332 (2022) - Emergency response provider and bystander good Samaritan civil immunity :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 42 - JUDICIARY AND JUDICIAL PROCEDURE Chapter 83 - Particular Rights and Immunities Section 8332 - Emergency response provider and bystander good Samaritan civil immunity Universal Citation: 42 PA Cons Stat § 8332 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 8332. Emergency response provider and bystander good Samaritan civil immunity. (a) General rule.-- Any person, including an emergency response provider, whether or not trained to practice medicine, who in good faith renders emergency care, treatment, first aid or rescue at the scene of an emergency event or crime or who moves the person receiving such care, first aid or rescue to a hospital or other place of medical care shall not be liable for any civil damages as a result of rendering such care, except in any act or omission intentionally designed to harm or any grossly negligent acts or omissions which result in harm to the person receiving emergency care or being moved to a hospital or other place of medical care. (b) Exceptions.-- (Deleted by amendment). (b.1) Injured police animals.-- A person, including an emergency response provider, whether trained to practice medicine, who, in good faith, renders emergency care, treatment, first aid or rescue to an injured police animal at the scene of an emergency event or crime or who moves an injured police animal receiving emergency care, treatment, first aid or rescue to a hospital or other place of medical care shall not be liable
perturbation.justification: The original contract language contained clear obligations with a specific deadline. The changed text significantly weakens these obligations, introducing ambiguity by using terms like 'endeavor,' 'consider,' and 'when feasible,' and replacing the hard deadline with a vague timeframe. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 pertains to 'Emergency response provider and bystander good Samaritan civil immunity' and is irrelevant to the contract's content, the Perturbation and Law Explanations both highlight that the changes undermine the enforceability of the contract, contradicting the principle that contractual terms should be clear and definite. This ambiguity makes it difficult to hold VerticalNet accountable, as the modified language allows them to potentially avoid fulfilling their obligations. This shift creates a significant legal contradiction by rendering the obligation vague and discretionary.

perturbation.original_text: VerticalNet shall from time to time provide or make available to Neoforma, for use in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement, the title and an abstract of (a) all original content created from time to time by the managing editor of the VerticalNet Medical Online Communities, and (b) the content created from time to time by guest columnists for the VerticalNet Medical Online Communities (the "VerticalNet Content"), to the extent such columnists have approved the provision of such content by VerticalNet to Neoforma.
perturbation.changed_text: VerticalNet may, at its discretion, occasionally provide or make vaguely available to Neoforma, for possible use according to a general interpretation of this Agreement, a summary of potentially relevant content created by select individuals associated with the VerticalNet Medical Online Communities (the "VerticalNet Content"), pending further review and subject to various undefined conditions.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a clear obligation for VerticalNet to provide content to Neoforma. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'shall' with 'may, at its discretion,' making the provision of content optional. It further weakens the obligation by using vague language like 'occasionally provide,' 'vaguely available,' 'possible use according to a general interpretation,' 'select individuals,' and 'various undefined conditions.'
perturbation.law_citation: Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes Title 42 Pa.C.S.A. § 8332. Enforcement of contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification undermines contract law in Pennsylvania by rendering the content provision obligation nearly unenforceable. The altered language gives VerticalNet broad discretion to avoid providing content to Neoforma, which contradicts the principle of definite and enforceable terms in contracts. This violates 42 Pa.C.S.A. § 8332 by making the contractual provision's enforcement uncertain and subject to the whim of one party.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text establishes a clear obligation for VerticalNet to provide content, while the modified text uses discretionary language ('may, at its discretion') and vague terms ('occasionally provide,' 'vaguely available,' 'possible use,' 'select individuals,' 'undefined conditions'). This undermines the enforceability of the content provision, effectively allowing VerticalNet to avoid its obligations. The Law Explanation accurately reflects that this violates contract law principles by making the contractual provision uncertain and subject to the whim of one party. Even without specific legal snippets, the shift from a mandatory 'shall' to a discretionary 'may' introduces significant ambiguity and weakens the contractual obligation to the point of near unenforceability. This aligns with the Ambiguity Legal Contradiction definition, as the altered language creates an ambiguous legal obligation, making it difficult to enforce and leaving the legal obligation open to dispute.

perturbation.original_text: Neoforma shall indemnify and hold harmless VerticalNet and its officers, directors, employees and agents from and against any and all losses, claims, damages, liabilities, obligations, penalties, judgments, awards, costs, expenses and disbursements, including without limitation, the costs, expenses and disbursements, as and when incurred, of investigating, preparing or defending any action, suit, proceeding or investigation asserted by a third party, caused by, relating to, based upon, arising out of or in connection with (a) any breach by Neoforma of the representations, warranties or agreements made by it under this Agreement, (b) negligence, recklessness or intentional misconduct on the part of Neoforma or its Affiliates or its officers, directors, employees, agents or consultants, (c) any claim that the Neoforma Content, Neoforma Product Listings, Neoforma Sites or a Neoforma Mark violates, infringes or misappropriates any Intellectual Property Rights or any other right of any third party, or (d) the use of a VerticalNet Mark or a VerticalNet Deliverable outside of the license rights granted herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Neoforma will consider indemnifying VerticalNet and its associated parties from certain potential losses, claims, damages, or liabilities, potentially including costs associated with investigating or defending against third-party actions, based on some connection to (a) a possible breach by Neoforma of its stated representations, warranties, or agreements under this Agreement, (b) questionable conduct by Neoforma or its Affiliates, officers, or consultants, (c) an alleged claim that Neoforma Content or Marks might infringe on Intellectual Property Rights, or (d) the permitted use of a VerticalNet Mark.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause establishes a clear indemnification obligation for Neoforma to protect VerticalNet from specific types of losses. The modified clause introduces significant ambiguity by replacing 'shall indemnify and hold harmless' with 'will consider indemnifying,' making the indemnification discretionary. It also uses vague and qualifying language such as 'certain potential losses,' 'potentially including,' 'some connection to,' 'possible breach,' 'questionable conduct,' 'alleged claim,' and 'might infringe.' It also limits the indemnification in subsection (d) to the 'permitted' use of the VerticalNet mark, where the original makes no such qualification.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract and Duty to Defend
perturbation.law_citation: Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes Title 42 Pa.C.S.A. § 8332. Enforcement of contracts, and Pennsylvania law regarding the Duty to Defend (See e.g., Erie Ins. Exch. v. Bristol Square Assocs., LP, 935 A.2d 131, 136 (Pa. Super. Ct. 2007))
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts contract law (42 Pa.C.S.A. § 8332) and established legal principles regarding the duty to defend in Pennsylvania. By making the indemnification discretionary ('will consider indemnifying'), Neoforma can avoid its contractual obligation to protect VerticalNet from covered claims. Furthermore, the vague language undermines the duty to defend, where under Pennsylvania law, the duty to defend is broader than the duty to indemnify. The altered language makes it nearly impossible for VerticalNet to rely on Neoforma for defense against third-party claims, substantially altering the intended risk allocation in the agreement.
perturbation.justification: The modified text fundamentally alters Neoforma's indemnification obligation from a mandatory duty to a discretionary consideration. The original text states Neoforma 'shall indemnify and hold harmless,' creating a clear legal obligation. The changed text uses 'will consider indemnifying,' making the indemnification optional. This introduces ambiguity, as Neoforma could argue it considered indemnifying VerticalNet but decided against it, thereby avoiding its contractual responsibility. The Law Explanation highlights that under Pennsylvania law, the duty to defend is broader than the duty to indemnify, further emphasizing the contradiction created by weakening the indemnification obligation.
